chapters.title: Two unlikely founders shaped by fractured childhoods
chapters.text:
The swagger Adam Neumann carried through high school did not come from birthright. He was raised by Avivit and Doron Neumann, physicians who crossed paths while studying medicine at Ben-Gurion University. Doron specialized in eye care; Avivit treated cancer patients. They wed in 1978, and Avivit delivered Adam in the desert city of Beersheba the following year at age twenty-two.

The marriage collapsed two weeks shy of its tenth anniversary, and Adam later called that breakup the hardest chapter of his youth. As Avivit shuttled Adam and his younger sister Adi between homes, resentment toward her hardened. Shortly after the divorce, mother and children relocated to Indianapolis so Avivit could finish her medical residency. For a boy already reeling, adapting to a foreign country without knowing English proved brutal — officials even tried dropping the final "n" from the family name. Avivit recalled watching her son unravel.

After two years in Indiana, the family moved back to Israel in 1990, settling in Nir Am, a small settlement a mile from Gaza amid desert stone, date palms, and pomegranate shrubs. Nir Am was a kibbutz — one of the socialist-Zionist communes that had spread across Israel in prior decades to build self-reliant agricultural settlements.

Miguel McKelvey's upbringing was equally unconventional. In the 1960s his mother Lucia lived in Taos, New Mexico, alongside three friends who all became mothers around the same time. McKelvey said the fathers drifted away one by one, and the women formed a matriarchal household. They maintained separate residences yet pooled resources to raise their children, inventing survival strategies outside mainstream family norms and conventional expectations. McKelvey later earned a perfect GPA studying architecture at the University of Oregon.

Gathering people in shared spaces can feel electric — and potentially profitable — especially as workers crave face-to-face connection in an increasingly virtual economy.

The formative years of WeWork's co-founders directly fueled the company's identity. Running a business or building one from scratch? The founders' triumphs and stumbles offer instructive lessons.

chapters.title: From a Brooklyn elevator ride to the birth of Green Desk
chapters.text:
While employed at the architectural firm JPDA in Brooklyn, McKelvey rode the subway to Tribeca to visit Gil Haklay, a fellow Israeli architect at the same company. At Haklay's building, a tall, loud, shirtless, shoeless man followed him into the elevator — striking even on a sweltering New York summer day. That eccentric figure was Haklay's roommate: Adam Neumann.

By coincidence, Neumann had recently relocated his struggling baby-clothing venture from a warehouse into a storefront at 68 Jay Street — the same Brooklyn building housing McKelvey's architecture office. The apparel business was failing, and McKelvey felt trapped constructing someone else's legacy rather than building his own. Shared proximity through Haklay sparked frequent conversations, and the two men soon began plotting a joint project while Neumann kept his clothing line on life support.

Their idea mirrored McKelvey's day job: leasing shared workspace to tenants who needed flexible offices. The catch was they owned no real estate of their own. Brainstorming a workaround, Neumann recalled vacant floors under renovation at 68 Jay Street and pitched landlord Joshua Guttman on converting one level into a communal office suite.

Guttman initially rejected the plan, but Neumann kept pressing until Guttman offered to show another property he had acquired nearby. When approval finally came, Guttman demanded a revenue-sharing arrangement on every lease. He also required a formal business plan — which sent Neumann racing to update McKelvey on the breakthrough.

WeWork would later describe itself as the world's largest physical network, calling its tenants "members" rather than ordinary renters.

Guttman funded the build-out while Neumann and McKelvey each chipped in $5,000 to launch construction. Neumann kept his clothing company running on the side; McKelvey went all-in on the new venture — Green Desk. Haklay eventually joined as a third partner, rounding out the founding team.

chapters.title: WeWork scaled fast as the founders recruited anyone within reach
chapters.text:
Green Desk delivered explosive returns, and by 2009 Neumann, McKelvey, and Haklay sold their stakes back to Guttman. Each founder pocketed roughly half a million dollars, paid in installments over several years. Haklay returned to Israel; Neumann and McKelvey reinvested as equal partners in a fresh venture called WeWork. Yet McKelvey's reserved temperament quickly gave way to Neumann speaking and behaving like the company's true proprietor.

While hunting for WeWork's inaugural property, the pair met Joel Schreiber, a young but seasoned real estate operator with deep ties in the city. Schreiber had no building to offer immediately, yet Neumann's intensity captivated him on the spot. After a single meeting, Schreiber committed $15 million to a company that barely existed on paper — an arbitrary bet on vision alone.

Neumann's theatrical style baffled early hires. WeWork's first offices were attractive but hardly revolutionary in design. Still, his gospel of community pulled talented people away from stable but dull careers at established firms. Unlike typical tech startups, WeWork offered neither generous salaries nor meaningful equity — the promise of belonging was the recruiting hook.

Danny Orenstein, McKelvey's high-school friend and an Oregon attorney, became general counsel, lending an "Esq." credential to official paperwork. Chia O'Keefe-Sally, McKelvey's half-sister from Georgia, helped open the first location beyond New York in San Francisco. Marga Snyder, formerly a concierge at the Iroquois hotel, took charge of events programming. Joey Cables, still in high school, served as the inaugural IT director.

Startup life is rarely glamorous, yet it delivers something corporate jobs often cannot: the feeling that you belong to a mission that might matter.

Neumann inserted himself into nearly every operational detail, personally signing off on floor plans for each new site. He also assumed the CEO mantle — crafting strategy, chasing partnerships, and building a New York reputation for pouring shots in meetings, first vodka, then tequila. McKelvey anchored the company in practicality while Neumann played mystic and deal-closer.

chapters.title: Expansion, ideology, and the reinvention of office culture
chapters.text:
Open-plan offices trace back to the Industrial Revolution, when Frederick Taylor's factory floors inspired white-collar layouts that resembled assembly lines. Frank Lloyd Wright's 1939 Johnson Wax Headquarters introduced warmer lighting and human scale to the workplace. Cubicles dominated the 1980s alongside desktop computers, until Silicon Valley startups demolished partitions, handed employees laptops, and furnished spaces with beanbags and foosball tables.

Each generation redesigns the workplace to match its own rhythms and cultural priorities.

Rival coworking operators lacked not only Neumann's ambition but also his access to capital. Joel Schreiber had delivered only a portion of his pledged investment — he never secured the full 33 percent stake promised. Even so, Neumann leveraged Schreiber's influential New York network once the financier bought into the vision. In summer 2011 Neumann rented a Hamptons property, telling staff the expense strained the budget but helped court wealthy contacts and deepen relationships. By 2012 he had raised roughly $7 million from friends and family.

While living in Los Angeles, Rebekah Neumann — Adam's wife — followed cousin Gwyneth Paltrow into Kabbalah, a hierarchical spiritual movement that elevated celebrities and the affluent. The Neumanns frequently sat at the head table beside Rabbi Eitan Yardeni during Friday meals with Kabbalah's VIP circle.

Neumann has an endless faith in his ability to convince people to do things he wants them to do. ~ Miguel McKelvey

Raised in secular Israel, Neumann had never embraced religious observance — his parents skipped holidays and Shabbat, and he resented Sabbath bus closures that blocked beach trips on Saturdays. Yet as his New York decade of false starts ended, Kabbalah supplied structure, meaning, and a lens for interpreting the world around him.

chapters.title: Neumann's outsized ego became WeWork's deepest liability
chapters.text:
Neumann framed WeWork's mounting expansion costs as a straightforward math problem with an easy answer. Tell investors how fast to grow, he argued, and WeWork would oblige — demand for desks was so intense that capital, not customers, was the only bottleneck to opening new locations.

SoftBank, a Japanese conglomerate with a modest U.S. venture arm, considered backing the company. A New York-based SoftBank investor flagged WeWork as exactly the kind of outsized bet founder Masayoshi Son favored. SoftBank would eventually pour enormous sums into the startup, becoming one of its heaviest backers.

Adam Neumann is WeWork. He is one-quarter crazy, one-quarter brilliant, and the other half is a fight between his ego and genuinely caring for people. ~ Francis Lobo

Following the $10 billion Series E round, Neumann told the Wall Street Journal that WeWork was profitable, needed no further funding before its IPO, and had hit every growth target — though he declined specifics. Every statement proved false. As a private company with Neumann controlling the board, little restrained him from saying whatever served the moment.

Salesmanship demands confidence, but an ego that dwarfs judgment can poison leadership and derail an organization.

Around this period WeWork signed a high-profile arrangement with British newspaper The Guardian. While WeWork renovated its sixth headquarters at 222 Broadway, both organizations temporarily shared the building for several months. After closing a new deal, Neumann rallied WeWork staff downstairs with free wine and blaring music — infuriating Guardian employees working quietly on the floor above. The clash nearly escalated into a fistfight between the two companies' workers, with Neumann cheering his side on.

Neumann later admitted his ego problems intensified during this stretch. It had always been formidable, but now he had genuine achievements to fuel it.

chapters.title: As WeWork's momentum collapsed, Neumann was pushed out
chapters.text:
No matter how much authority Neumann delegated to McKelvey, Artie, or others, WeWork remained his creation. The entire organization orbited his personality, and the same magnetism that seduced investors captivated employees. Neumann commanded rooms through sheer physical presence and rhetorical force.

When (Neumann) looks at you, it's what I imagine it feels like to have Julius Caesar stare at you; there's an intensity, a self-understanding, a belief. ~ Alana Anderson

Staff recognized a familiar pattern. New hires arrived energized for six to nine months, then enthusiasm eroded until the eighteen-month mark, when skepticism set in. Minor grievances — like the company's meat ban — could trigger departures. WeWork replaced them, and the cycle restarted.

For many insiders, WeWork felt less like a corporation than a cult built around its founder.

In autumn 2018 Neumann vacationed in Hawaii, confident SoftBank would deliver a $20 billion infusion dubbed Project Fortitude. WeWork planned to open seventy-nine locations in December alone — more than its first six years combined — including a Salesforce Tower headquarters overlooking San Francisco. Though a One World Trade Center deal had collapsed, WeWork had overtaken JPMorgan as New York City's largest office tenant.

By 2018, criticism of management excess and unchecked personal spending mounted from multiple directions. Wingspan, Rebekah Neumann's side venture, surfaced similar red flags. The accumulating scandal blocked WeWork's IPO and forced Neumann's resignation as CEO.

Stripped of the company he had poured more than a decade into, Neumann reportedly retreated to Gramercy with a desk note listing three lessons: Listen. Always arrive on time. Be a decent coworker.

Did you know? According to techjury, despite its recent financial crisis, and with revenue of nearly $3.81 billion in 2020, WeWork was still going strong.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
WeWork's greatest assets and its gravest flaws both flowed from the top. Neumann was an extraordinary pitchman, and the personality cult surrounding him kept the machine humming. Yet that same dynamic silenced dissent — his promises to investors, landlords, and staff were the fuel, and also the fiction, powering the enterprise.

WeWork's rise didn't shock Schwartz, who had spent part of his career in finance. This was how the system worked. Adam had persuaded one investor after another to believe in his vision. ~ Reeves Wiedeman

Neumann built the quintessential venture of the 2010s: packing vacant commercial real estate with freelancers, then convincing corporations they needed the same communal energy. He rode a global flood of cheap capital that rewarded audacity over arithmetic. After years of ascent, forces larger than charisma exposed the contradiction at WeWork's core.

Drawing a single moral from Neumann's arc proved elusive. His story warned about behaviors modern capitalism celebrates, the venture ecosystem's excesses, and timeless dangers of hubris — yet it also sketched a blueprint for a particular kind of triumph. One prominent Silicon Valley investor reportedly gauged founders by their take on Neumann; the winning answer acknowledged his flaws while praising what he built.

Try this

Everyone chases excellence and success, yet even those who reach the summit can stumble without deliberate "success management." Whether you are launching a venture or leading an established one, pair boldness with measured restraint — and surround yourself with partners or subordinates willing to call you out when ego outruns judgment.
